---
title: "Adding and Scaling: The Linear Combination"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# One restrained visual style for every figure in this article, matched to the
# rest of the Geometry-of-Data track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

VEC    = "#2b3a67"   # the input vectors
SCALED = "#c1121f"   # scaled copies / the result
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"   # highlights / sums / means
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PALE   = "#9fb3d1"


def draw_vec(ax, tail, head, color=VEC, lw=2.4, label=None, z=5,
             label_dx=0.0, label_dy=0.0):
    """Draw a 2-D arrow from tail to head with a clean arrowhead."""
    ax.annotate("", xy=head, xytext=tail,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0), zorder=z)
    if label is not None:
        ax.text(head[0] + label_dx, head[1] + label_dy, label,
                color=color, fontsize=12, ha="left", va="bottom")
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the second article in the **Geometry of
> Data** track. It assumes only [GEOM-00 *What Is a Vector, Really?*], which fixed the
> object we work with: a vector is at once an arrow from the origin, an ordered list of
> numbers in $\mathbb{R}^n$, and one row of a data table. GEOM-00 left a debt — it
> repeatedly *used* "componentwise addition" and "scaling" to decompose a vector into its
> coordinates, while promising those two operations would be defined and drawn here. This
> article pays that debt in full. It defines the **only two operations** linear algebra
> grants a vector, draws each one, fuses them into the single move from which the rest of
> the subject is assembled — the **linear combination** — and states the eight rules those
> operations obey, not as sacred axioms but as the bookkeeping that makes the geometry
> work. It sets up [GEOM-02 *Span and Subspaces*] (turn the coefficients of a linear
> combination loose and ask what you can reach) and [GEOM-08 *Matrices as Linear Maps*]
> (a matrix acts on a vector by taking a linear combination of its columns).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Linear algebra permits a vector exactly two operations: **add two vectors**, and **scale
one vector by a number**. That is the entire vocabulary — every later construction in this
track, however elaborate, is built from just these two moves applied over and over. This
article defines both operations *componentwise* (slot by slot) and shows that each has a
clean geometric picture: addition is tip-to-tail (equivalently, the diagonal of a
parallelogram), and scaling is stretching, shrinking, or flipping an arrow along its own
line. We then fuse the two into the **linear combination**
$c_1\mathbf{v}_1 + \cdots + c_k\mathbf{v}_k$ — scale each vector, then add the results —
and argue that this single expression is the load-bearing idea of the whole subject. We
close by stating the eight algebraic rules these operations satisfy, deriving each from the
componentwise definitions, and reading them not as abstract axioms but as the precise
guarantees that let us manipulate vectors as freely as numbers. The single claim the
article defends: **two operations, honestly understood, are enough to generate all of
linear algebra — and a weighted average is nothing more than a linear combination whose
coefficients happen to sum to one.**

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
There are only **two** things you may do to vectors: **add** them, and **scale** them by a
number. Both are done one coordinate at a time, and both have a picture — addition lays
arrows tip-to-tail; scaling stretches or flips an arrow along its own line. Glue the two
together and you get the **linear combination** $c_1\mathbf{v}_1 + \cdots + c_k\mathbf{v}_k$:
scale each vector by its own coefficient, then sum. This one expression is the atom of the
entire track — span, projection, matrix multiplication, and least squares are all linear
combinations in disguise. The eight algebraic "rules of a vector space" are not extra
content to memorize; they are simply the slot-by-slot arithmetic of real numbers,
inherited one coordinate at a time.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The destination — the linear combination — looks almost
too simple to deserve an article. The point of the article is that *almost everything else
in the track is this expression wearing a costume*, so understanding it cleanly, with both
its algebra and its picture, is what makes the later machinery feel inevitable instead of
arbitrary. When matrix multiplication arrives in [GEOM-09] and looks like a strange
row-times-column ritual, the antidote will be: *it is taking linear combinations of
columns*, the move defined right here.

# Why we are here: the problem that forced these two operations {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **why these two operations, and why only
these two?** A vector, GEOM-00 insisted, is a point in space and a row of data. Of all the
things one could imagine doing to such an object, linear algebra is built on a deliberately
*tiny* toolkit — add, and scale — and the power of the subject comes precisely from that
restraint. Understanding *why* the toolkit is so small is understanding what "linear" means.

## What "linear" is actually promising

The word **linear** is doing real work, and it is worth saying what it promises before we
formalize anything. A relationship is linear when **the whole is the sum of its parts, each
part scaled by how much of it you have.** A grocery bill is linear: three apples at \$0.50
and two loaves at \$2.00 cost $3 \times 0.50 + 2 \times 2.00$ — you *scale* each unit price
by a quantity and *add*. Mixing paint is linear: two parts blue and one part yellow is
$2 \cdot (\text{blue}) + 1 \cdot (\text{yellow})$, scaling each color by its share and
combining. A portfolio's value is linear in its holdings; a weighted exam grade is linear
in its components.

Every one of those everyday computations is the *same* shape: take some base quantities,
scale each by a number, add the scaled results. Linear algebra is the study of that shape,
made precise and lifted into $n$ dimensions.

This is also, historically, the order in which the subject was discovered. People computed
*with* "scale and add" — on forces, on velocities, on systems of linear equations — for
centuries before anyone wrote down what the operations *were*. Hermann Grassmann's 1844
*Ausdehnungslehre* ("theory of extension") was the first attempt to treat adding and
scaling abstract magnitudes as the primitive operations of a self-contained calculus; it
was so far ahead of its notation that almost no one read it. It took until Giuseppe Peano's
1888 *Calcolo geometrico* for the modern list — the eight rules of @thm-vector-space below —
to be written out as the defining properties of what he called a "linear system." The
striking historical fact is the *direction* of the discovery: the axioms came **last**, as a
distillation of operations practitioners had trusted for generations, not first as
postulates handed down. That is exactly the stance this article takes in @sec-rules — the
eight rules are bookkeeping we *verify*, not commandments we obey. The two operations — scaling and adding — are
exactly the two verbs in that sentence, and the reason the toolkit is only two operations
wide is that **this shape is the entire subject.** There is no third verb because "scale
and add" already captures what "linear" means. Anything you cannot express by scaling and
adding is, by definition, *non*linear, and lives outside this subject (it is why squaring a
feature, or multiplying two features together, immediately takes you out of plain linear
algebra and into the harder territory the later tracks handle).

## Why a data scientist should care

GEOM-00 turned a data table into a cloud of points. But a cloud you can only *look* at is
inert; to *do* anything with it you need operations, and the two defined here are the ones
that turn out to matter most. Three concrete jobs, each one a linear combination:

- **Combining features.** A credit score, a risk index, a "health score" from a fitness
  tracker — these are almost always a weighted sum of measured features:
  $\text{score} = w_1 \cdot \text{feature}_1 + \cdots + w_p \cdot \text{feature}_p$. That
  *is* a linear combination of the feature values, with the weights as coefficients. The
  whole of linear regression ([REGR-01], [REGR-05]) is the search for the best such weights.
- **Averaging observations.** The "typical" customer, the centroid of a cluster, the mean
  image of a face — each is an average of data-row vectors, which we will see (@sec-weighted)
  is a linear combination whose coefficients are all $1/n$.
- **Moving through space.** A neural network's hidden layer, a change of coordinates, a
  rotation — each acts by forming linear combinations of its inputs. When [GEOM-08] reveals
  that *every* matrix does its work by taking linear combinations of columns, this article
  is the thing that makes the statement land.

Hold this *why* as we go: every formal step below is in service of pinning down the one
operation that all of these share, so that when it reappears in a dozen disguises later in
the track, you recognize it on sight.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why does linear algebra only allow adding and scaling — why can't I multiply two vectors
together?"* Because **"linear" is exactly the restriction to adding and scaling**, and that
restriction is a feature, not a limitation. The honest, layered answer an interviewer wants:
**(1)** You *can* define products of vectors, and we will — the dot product ([GEOM-04]) takes
two vectors and returns a *number*, and there is a cross product in $\mathbb{R}^3$ — but
neither is part of the vector-space structure itself; they are extra structure laid on top.
**(2)** A componentwise product $(x_1 y_1, \dots, x_n y_n)$ exists too (NumPy's `*`), but it
breaks the geometry: it depends on the choice of axes in a way that addition and scaling do
not, so it is not a meaningful geometric operation on the underlying arrows. **(3)** The
deep reason: the moment you allow products of the *variables*, you leave linearity behind —
$x^2$ and $x_1 x_2$ are nonlinear — and you lose the closed-form, picture-able theory this
entire track is built on. Linear algebra is powerful *because* it is restrictive.
:::

# Intuition first: two pictures, before any symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before a single formula, get the two pictures in hand. They are the entire geometric
content of the article; the algebra that follows is just these pictures written in
coordinates.

## Picture one — adding is walking tip-to-tail

To add two arrows, **walk the first, then walk the second starting from where the first
ended.** Place the tail of $\mathbf{w}$ at the tip of $\mathbf{v}$; the sum
$\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{w}$ is the single arrow from the original start to the final finish.
This is the *tip-to-tail* rule, and it is just the obvious fact that displacements
accumulate: go $3$ steps east then $2$ steps north, and your net displacement is the
straight arrow to where you stand.

There is a second, equivalent picture. If instead you draw both arrows from the *same*
starting point, they form two sides of a **parallelogram**, and the sum is its diagonal.
The two pictures agree because the parallelogram's far side is just $\mathbf{w}$ slid over
to begin at $\mathbf{v}$'s tip — the tip-to-tail walk traced along the top edge. We draw
both in @fig-add and verify they land on the same point.

## Picture two — scaling slides along a line

To scale an arrow by a number $c$, **stretch it to $c$ times its length, keeping its
direction** — unless $c$ is negative, in which case you also flip it to point the opposite
way. Doubling ($c=2$) makes the arrow twice as long; halving ($c=\tfrac12$) shrinks it;
$c=-1$ reverses it; $c=0$ collapses it to the origin. Crucially, scaling never lets the
arrow leave the **line through the origin that it already points along** — every scaled
copy, for every $c$, lies on that one line. That single line, swept out as $c$ ranges over
all reals, is our first glimpse of a *span* (the subject of [GEOM-02]).

```{python}
#| label: fig-add
#| fig-cap: "**Left:** addition as a tip-to-tail walk. Slide the tail of $\\mathbf{w}=(1,2)$ to the tip of $\\mathbf{v}=(3,1)$ (faint copy); the sum $\\mathbf{v}+\\mathbf{w}=(4,3)$ is the arrow from the common origin to where the walk ends. Drawing $\\mathbf{w}$ first and then $\\mathbf{v}$ (the other faint path) lands on the very same point — addition does not care about order. **Right:** scaling slides an arrow along its own line through the origin. Every scalar multiple $c\\mathbf{v}$ of $\\mathbf{v}=(2,1)$ — here $c=2,\\,1,\\,\\tfrac12,\\,-1$ — lies on one line; $c>1$ stretches, $0<c<1$ shrinks, $c<0$ flips to the other side of the origin."
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 4.2))

# --- Left: tip-to-tail and the parallelogram ---
v = np.array([3, 1]); w = np.array([1, 2]); s = v + w
draw_vec(axL, (0, 0), v, color=VEC, label=r"$\mathbf{v}$", label_dx=0.05, label_dy=-0.25)
draw_vec(axL, (0, 0), w, color=ACCENT, label=r"$\mathbf{w}$", label_dx=-0.55, label_dy=0.05)
# w slid to the tip of v (faint), and v slid to the tip of w (faint): parallelogram
draw_vec(axL, v, s, color=ACCENT, lw=1.6, z=3)
axL.annotate("", xy=s, xytext=tuple(w),
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=VEC, lw=1.6, alpha=0.5,
                             shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0), zorder=3)
draw_vec(axL, (0, 0), s, color=SCALED, lw=2.6,
         label=r"$\mathbf{v}+\mathbf{w}=(4,3)$", label_dx=0.05, label_dy=0.1)
axL.scatter([0], [0], color="black", s=25, zorder=6)
axL.set_xlim(-0.5, 5); axL.set_ylim(-0.5, 4)
axL.set_aspect("equal")
axL.set_xlabel("axis 1"); axL.set_ylabel("axis 2")
axL.set_title("Addition: tip-to-tail = parallelogram diagonal")

# --- Right: scalar multiples on one line ---
vv = np.array([2, 1])
line_t = np.linspace(-1.6, 2.4, 50)
axR.plot(line_t * vv[0], line_t * vv[1], color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.0, zorder=1)
for c, col, lab in [(2, SCALED, r"$2\mathbf{v}$"),
                    (1, VEC, r"$\mathbf{v}$"),
                    (0.5, ACCENT, r"$\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{v}$"),
                    (-1, PALE, r"$-\mathbf{v}$")]:
    draw_vec(axR, (0, 0), c * vv, color=col, lw=2.4,
             label=lab, label_dx=0.08, label_dy=0.04)
axR.scatter([0], [0], color="black", s=25, zorder=6)
axR.set_xlim(-3, 5); axR.set_ylim(-2, 3)
axR.set_aspect("equal")
axR.set_xlabel("axis 1"); axR.set_ylabel("axis 2")
axR.set_title("Scaling: every $c\\mathbf{v}$ lies on one line")
plt.tight_layout()
```

Those two pictures — *walk arrows tip-to-tail*, *slide an arrow along its line* — are all
the geometry there is. The rest of this article makes them exact in coordinates, fuses
them, and audits the rules they obey.

# Formal development {#sec-formal}

We work in $\mathbb{R}^n$, the space of ordered lists of $n$ real numbers fixed in
[GEOM-00, @def-rn]. Recall the conventions we keep: vectors are lowercase boldface
$\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w}, \mathbf{x}$; their components carry a subscript, so
$\mathbf{v} = (v_1, \dots, v_n)$, written as a column when it matters; and plain lowercase
letters $c, d, \lambda$ are **scalars** — single real numbers. Both operations below are
defined **componentwise**, meaning *slot by slot*: we say what happens in coordinate $i$,
for each $i$ from $1$ to $n$, and that defines the whole vector.

## Vector addition {#sec-addition}

::: {#def-addition}
**Definition (vector addition).** For two vectors $\mathbf{v} = (v_1, \dots, v_n)$ and
$\mathbf{w} = (w_1, \dots, w_n)$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, their **sum** is the vector obtained by
adding corresponding components:
$$
\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{w}
= \begin{bmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \\ \vdots \\ v_n \end{bmatrix}
+ \begin{bmatrix} w_1 \\ w_2 \\ \vdots \\ w_n \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} v_1 + w_1 \\ v_2 + w_2 \\ \vdots \\ v_n + w_n \end{bmatrix}.
$$ {#eq-addition}
The sum is again a vector in $\mathbb{R}^n$ — addition takes two vectors and returns one of
the same size.
:::

Read @eq-addition for exactly what it says: the $i$-th slot of the sum is $v_i + w_i$, the
ordinary sum of two real numbers, and nothing crosses between slots — coordinate $1$ never
talks to coordinate $2$. Two demands are hidden in the definition and worth surfacing. First,
the two vectors must have the **same number of components**; you cannot add a vector in
$\mathbb{R}^3$ to one in $\mathbb{R}^5$, because slot $4$ has no partner. Second, the
operation is built entirely on the addition of *real numbers* — which is why, as we will see
in @sec-rules, every algebraic rule of vector addition is just the corresponding rule for
real numbers, applied in every slot at once.

Now connect @eq-addition to the tip-to-tail picture of @fig-add, because the definition
should not feel disconnected from the drawing. In $\mathbb{R}^2$, the arrow $\mathbf{v} =
(v_1, v_2)$ means "go $v_1$ right and $v_2$ up." Walking $\mathbf{w} = (w_1, w_2)$ from
there means "go a further $w_1$ right and $w_2$ up." Your total horizontal travel is
$v_1 + w_1$ and your total vertical travel is $v_2 + w_2$ — which is exactly the sum vector
$(v_1 + w_1,\, v_2 + w_2)$ of @eq-addition. **The componentwise rule and the tip-to-tail
walk are the same statement**: displacements add coordinate by coordinate because that is
what "and then keep going" does to each axis independently.

A concrete instance, the one drawn in @fig-add:
$$
\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{w}
= \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 3 + 1 \\ 1 + 2 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

## Scalar multiplication {#sec-scaling}

::: {#def-scaling}
**Definition (scalar multiplication).** For a scalar $c \in \mathbb{R}$ and a vector
$\mathbf{v} = (v_1, \dots, v_n)$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, the **scalar multiple** $c\mathbf{v}$ is
obtained by multiplying every component by $c$:
$$
c\,\mathbf{v}
= c \begin{bmatrix} v_1 \\ v_2 \\ \vdots \\ v_n \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} c\,v_1 \\ c\,v_2 \\ \vdots \\ c\,v_n \end{bmatrix}.
$$ {#eq-scaling}
The result is again a vector in $\mathbb{R}^n$.
:::

Again the rule is slot-by-slot: scale every coordinate by the same number $c$. The
geometric reading matches the right panel of @fig-add. Multiplying $(v_1, v_2)$ by $c$ sends
it to $(cv_1, cv_2)$ — every coordinate grows (or shrinks) by the same factor, so the arrow
keeps its direction and only changes length, *unless* $c < 0$, when every coordinate flips
sign and the arrow reverses. Three special values are worth naming because they recur:

- $c = 0$ gives $0\mathbf{v} = (0, \dots, 0) = \mathbf{0}$, the zero vector — scaling by
  zero collapses any arrow to the origin. (We prove this is forced, not just defined, in
  @sec-rules.)
- $c = 1$ gives $1\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}$ — scaling by one changes nothing.
- $c = -1$ gives $-\mathbf{v} = (-v_1, \dots, -v_n)$, the **negative** of $\mathbf{v}$: the
  same arrow pointing the opposite way. This is what lets us *subtract*:
  $\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{w}$ is shorthand for $\mathbf{v} + (-1)\mathbf{w}$, addition of the
  flipped vector. So subtraction is not a third operation — it is addition and scaling
  combined, our first hint that everything reduces to the two.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is vector subtraction a separate operation?"* No, and saying so cleanly shows you
understand the structure. $\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{w}$ is *defined* as
$\mathbf{v} + (-1)\mathbf{w}$ — scale $\mathbf{w}$ by $-1$, then add. Geometrically,
$\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{w}$ is the arrow that points **from the tip of $\mathbf{w}$ to the tip
of $\mathbf{v}$** (slide it to the origin and it is the difference), which is why the
distance between two data points is the length of their difference vector — a fact we will
lean on constantly once [GEOM-04] gives us length. The takeaway: the "two operations" of the
Core really are only two; subtraction, averaging, and every other manipulation are built
from them.
:::

## The linear combination: fusing the two operations {#sec-lincomb}

We have addition and we have scaling. The single object the rest of the track is built from
is what you get when you use **both at once** on a collection of vectors.

::: {#def-lincomb}
**Definition (linear combination).** Given vectors $\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \dots,
\mathbf{v}_k$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ and scalars $c_1, c_2, \dots, c_k \in \mathbb{R}$ (the
**coefficients** or **weights**), the **linear combination** of the $\mathbf{v}_i$ with
those coefficients is the vector
$$
c_1 \mathbf{v}_1 + c_2 \mathbf{v}_2 + \cdots + c_k \mathbf{v}_k
= \sum_{i=1}^{k} c_i \mathbf{v}_i.
$$ {#eq-lincomb}
:::

Read the recipe in @eq-lincomb literally, because it is *only* the two operations in
sequence: **scale each vector $\mathbf{v}_i$ by its coefficient $c_i$** (that is
@eq-scaling, done $k$ times), **then add the scaled results** (that is @eq-addition, done
$k-1$ times). Nothing else. A linear combination is the *compound* move; addition and
scaling are its two primitive halves.

Let us make the componentwise form explicit so there is no mystery about what
@eq-lincomb produces. Writing each $\mathbf{v}_i = (v_{1i}, v_{2i}, \dots, v_{ni})$ — where
$v_{ji}$ is "coordinate $j$ of the $i$-th vector" — and applying the two definitions slot by
slot, the $j$-th coordinate of the linear combination is
$$
\bigl(c_1\mathbf{v}_1 + \cdots + c_k\mathbf{v}_k\bigr)_j
= c_1 v_{j1} + c_2 v_{j2} + \cdots + c_k v_{jk}
= \sum_{i=1}^{k} c_i\, v_{ji}.
$$ {#eq-lincomb-comp}
Each coordinate of the result is itself an ordinary weighted sum of numbers. That is the
whole content; @fig-lincomb walks one such combination geometrically.

```{python}
#| label: fig-lincomb
#| fig-cap: "A linear combination is *scale each, then add*. Starting from $\\mathbf{v}_1=(2,0)$ and $\\mathbf{v}_2=(1,2)$, form $1.5\\,\\mathbf{v}_1 + 1.0\\,\\mathbf{v}_2$. **Step 1 (faint):** scale — $\\mathbf{v}_1$ stretches to $1.5\\mathbf{v}_1=(3,0)$, $\\mathbf{v}_2$ stays as $1.0\\mathbf{v}_2=(1,2)$. **Step 2:** add the two scaled arrows tip-to-tail, giving $(4,2)$ (red). Componentwise this is $(1.5\\cdot2 + 1\\cdot1,\\ 1.5\\cdot0 + 1\\cdot2)=(4,2)$ — the picture and @eq-lincomb-comp agree."
v1 = np.array([2, 0]); v2 = np.array([1, 2])
c1, c2 = 1.5, 1.0
sv1, sv2 = c1 * v1, c2 * v2
res = sv1 + sv2

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.6))
# originals
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), v1, color=VEC, lw=1.6, label=r"$\mathbf{v}_1$", label_dy=-0.28)
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), v2, color=ACCENT, lw=1.6, label=r"$\mathbf{v}_2$", label_dx=-0.55)
# scaled copies (faint)
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), sv1, color=VEC, lw=2.6, z=4,
         label=r"$1.5\,\mathbf{v}_1$", label_dy=-0.3)
# v2 scaled equals v2 here (c2=1); show it slid to the tip of sv1 for the add
ax.annotate("", xy=tuple(res), xytext=tuple(sv1),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ACCENT, lw=2.6,
                            shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0), zorder=4)
ax.text(sv1[0] + 0.05, (sv1[1] + res[1]) / 2, r"$+\,1.0\,\mathbf{v}_2$",
        color=ACCENT, fontsize=11)
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), res, color=SCALED, lw=2.8,
         label=r"$1.5\mathbf{v}_1+1.0\mathbf{v}_2=(4,2)$", label_dx=0.05, label_dy=0.12)
ax.scatter([0], [0], color="black", s=25, zorder=6)
ax.set_xlim(-0.5, 5); ax.set_ylim(-0.5, 3)
ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.set_xlabel("axis 1"); ax.set_ylabel("axis 2")
ax.set_title("Linear combination: scale each, then add")
plt.tight_layout()
```

Why does this deserve to be called *the* atom of the subject? Because the headline results
of the entire track are linear combinations under other names, and naming them now means we
will recognize them on sight:

- **Coordinates** are a linear combination. GEOM-00's @eq-coords showed every vector is
  $\mathbf{x} = x_1\mathbf{e}_1 + \cdots + x_n\mathbf{e}_n$ — a linear combination of the
  axis vectors with the coordinates as coefficients. We promised there to define the
  operations used; @def-addition and @def-scaling are that promise kept.
- **Span** ([GEOM-02]) is the set of *all* linear combinations of some vectors — turn the
  coefficients loose and collect everywhere you can reach.
- **Matrix–vector products** ([GEOM-08]). The product $A\mathbf{x}$ is exactly the linear
  combination of $A$'s columns with the entries of $\mathbf{x}$ as coefficients. The whole
  of "what a matrix does" is "it forms linear combinations of its columns."
- **A fitted regression** is a linear combination of predictor columns; a **principal
  component** is a linear combination of features. The list does not stop.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What is a linear combination, in one sentence, and why does it matter so much?"* "Scale
each of several vectors by a number and add the results." Why it matters: it is the *only*
way the two permitted operations can be combined, so **anything** linear algebra can build
from a set of vectors is a linear combination of them — there is literally nothing else to
make. That is why span, matrix products, projections, regressions, and PCA all reduce to
this one expression. An interviewer raising this is checking whether you see linear algebra
as a pile of separate tricks or as repeated application of one move; the right answer is the
latter.
:::

# The eight rules, as the bookkeeping of two operations {#sec-rules}

Textbooks define a "vector space" by eight axioms and present them with some ceremony. We
take the track's stance ([GEOM-00, @sec-failure]): these are **not** sacred axioms handed
down to be revered — they are simply the algebraic guarantees that our two componentwise
operations *happen to satisfy*, inherited one coordinate at a time from the ordinary
arithmetic of real numbers. We will *state* all eight and *prove* the representative ones
directly from @def-addition and @def-scaling, so you see they are facts, not assumptions.
Their job is practical: they are exactly the permissions that let us push vector expressions
around — regroup, reorder, factor, cancel — as freely as we push numbers around. Without
them, an expression like $c_1\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2\mathbf{v}_2$ would have no guaranteed meaning
when we rearrange it.

Throughout, $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and $c, d \in \mathbb{R}$.

::: {#thm-vector-space}
**The eight rules.** Vector addition and scalar multiplication on $\mathbb{R}^n$ satisfy:

*Rules of addition.*
1. **Commutativity:** $\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w} + \mathbf{v}$.
2. **Associativity:** $(\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}) + \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{u} + (\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{w})$.
3. **Additive identity:** there is a vector $\mathbf{0}$ with $\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{0} = \mathbf{v}$ for all $\mathbf{v}$.
4. **Additive inverse:** every $\mathbf{v}$ has a vector $-\mathbf{v}$ with $\mathbf{v} + (-\mathbf{v}) = \mathbf{0}$.

*Rules of scaling, and how the two operations interact.*
5. **Distributivity over vector sums:** $c(\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{w}) = c\mathbf{v} + c\mathbf{w}$.
6. **Distributivity over scalar sums:** $(c + d)\mathbf{v} = c\mathbf{v} + d\mathbf{v}$.
7. **Associativity of scaling:** $c(d\mathbf{v}) = (cd)\mathbf{v}$.
8. **Scalar identity:** $1\,\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Every proof is the same idea: *go to coordinate $j$, where everything is real numbers, use
the matching rule for real numbers, and note it held in every slot.* We prove four — one
addition rule, both distributive laws, and the scaling associativity — which between them
exhibit every technique; the rest are identical in form.

**Rule 1 (commutativity of addition).** Look at the $j$-th coordinate of each side. By
@eq-addition, coordinate $j$ of $\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{w}$ is $v_j + w_j$, and coordinate $j$
of $\mathbf{w} + \mathbf{v}$ is $w_j + v_j$. But $v_j$ and $w_j$ are real numbers, and real
addition is commutative, so $v_j + w_j = w_j + v_j$. The two vectors agree in every
coordinate $j = 1, \dots, n$, so they are the same vector. (This is the *tip-to-tail order
does not matter* fact drawn in the left panel of @fig-add — both walks reach $(4,3)$.)

**Rule 5 (distributing a scalar over a vector sum).** Take coordinate $j$ of the left side,
$c(\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{w})$. By @eq-addition the inner sum has $j$-th coordinate
$v_j + w_j$; then by @eq-scaling, multiplying by $c$ gives
$$
\bigl[c(\mathbf{v}+\mathbf{w})\bigr]_j = c\,(v_j + w_j).
$$
Coordinate $j$ of the right side, $c\mathbf{v} + c\mathbf{w}$, is (by @eq-scaling then
@eq-addition)
$$
\bigl[c\mathbf{v} + c\mathbf{w}\bigr]_j = c v_j + c w_j.
$$
The ordinary distributive law for real numbers says $c(v_j + w_j) = c v_j + c w_j$, so the
two coordinates are equal — for every $j$. Hence the two vectors are equal.

**Rule 6 (distributing over a sum of scalars).** Coordinate $j$ of $(c+d)\mathbf{v}$ is, by
@eq-scaling, $(c + d)v_j$. Coordinate $j$ of $c\mathbf{v} + d\mathbf{v}$ is $c v_j + d v_j$.
Real-number distributivity gives $(c+d)v_j = c v_j + d v_j$ in each slot, so the vectors
agree. (Geometrically this is "stretch by $c$, separately stretch by $d$, add — same as
stretch by $c+d$," because all three arrows lie on $\mathbf{v}$'s one line.)

**Rule 7 (associativity of scaling).** Coordinate $j$ of $c(d\mathbf{v})$ is, applying
@eq-scaling twice (inner first), $c\,(d v_j)$. Coordinate $j$ of $(cd)\mathbf{v}$ is
$(cd)v_j$. Real multiplication is associative, $c(d v_j) = (cd) v_j$, so the vectors are
equal in every slot.

The remaining rules go the same way. **Rule 2** is associativity of real addition in each
slot. **Rule 3** holds with $\mathbf{0} = (0, \dots, 0)$, since $v_j + 0 = v_j$ in each
slot. **Rule 4** holds with $-\mathbf{v} = (-1)\mathbf{v} = (-v_1, \dots, -v_n)$, since
$v_j + (-v_j) = 0$ in each slot. **Rule 8** is $1 \cdot v_j = v_j$ in each slot. In all
eight, the vector rule is the real-number rule applied coordinatewise. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The proof exposes the real content of @thm-vector-space: **$\mathbb{R}^n$ inherits its
algebra, slot by slot, from $\mathbb{R}$.** There is no new arithmetic to learn; there is
only old arithmetic done in parallel across $n$ coordinates. That is exactly why we can
manipulate vector expressions with the same reflexes we use on numbers — and it is the
license behind every algebraic rearrangement in the rest of the track.

Two small but genuinely useful facts fall straight out of these rules, and we derive them
rather than assert them, since they will be used silently later.

::: {#lem-consequences}
**Lemma (forced consequences).** For every $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and every
$c \in \mathbb{R}$:
$$
\text{(a)}\quad 0\,\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}, \qquad\qquad
\text{(b)}\quad (-1)\,\mathbf{v} = -\mathbf{v}, \qquad\qquad
\text{(c)}\quad c\,\mathbf{0} = \mathbf{0}.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
**(a)** This *looks* obvious from @eq-scaling, but the point is that it is *forced* by the
rules even without peeking at coordinates — which is what makes it true in any vector space.
Start from the real-number fact $0 = 0 + 0$ and apply Rule 6 (distributivity over a sum of
scalars) with $c = d = 0$:
$$
0\,\mathbf{v} = (0 + 0)\,\mathbf{v} = 0\,\mathbf{v} + 0\,\mathbf{v}.
$$
Now $0\mathbf{v}$ is some vector; call it $\mathbf{a}$, so $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{a} +
\mathbf{a}$. Add its inverse $-\mathbf{a}$ (which exists by Rule 4) to both sides and use
Rules 2, 4, 3 in turn:
$$
\mathbf{a} + (-\mathbf{a}) = (\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{a}) + (-\mathbf{a})
= \mathbf{a} + \bigl(\mathbf{a} + (-\mathbf{a})\bigr)
\;\Longrightarrow\; \mathbf{0} = \mathbf{a} + \mathbf{0} = \mathbf{a}.
$$
So $\mathbf{a} = 0\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$. Scaling by zero must collapse to the origin — it
is not a separate decree but a consequence of distributivity.

**(b)** We show $(-1)\mathbf{v}$ is *the* additive inverse of $\mathbf{v}$. Using Rule 8
($1\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}$), then Rule 6, then part (a):
$$
\mathbf{v} + (-1)\mathbf{v} = 1\mathbf{v} + (-1)\mathbf{v} = (1 + (-1))\mathbf{v}
= 0\,\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}.
$$
Since adding $(-1)\mathbf{v}$ to $\mathbf{v}$ yields $\mathbf{0}$, by definition
$(-1)\mathbf{v}$ is the inverse $-\mathbf{v}$. This is why "flip the arrow" (the picture) and
"multiply by $-1$" (the algebra) are the same thing.

**(c)** From $\mathbf{0} = \mathbf{0} + \mathbf{0}$ (Rule 3) and Rule 5 (distributing a
scalar over a sum): $c\mathbf{0} = c(\mathbf{0} + \mathbf{0}) = c\mathbf{0} + c\mathbf{0}$,
and the same cancellation as in (a) gives $c\mathbf{0} = \mathbf{0}$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why bother with the eight axioms — isn't $\mathbb{R}^n$ obviously well-behaved?"* The
honest answer has two layers. **First**, for $\mathbb{R}^n$ the rules really are "obvious"
in the sense we just proved — they descend coordinatewise from real arithmetic, so you will
never have to *check* them for ordinary numeric vectors. **Second**, and this is the reason
the axioms are stated abstractly at all: the *same eight rules* are satisfied by objects
that are not tuples of numbers — polynomials, continuous functions, random variables,
images. Anything obeying these eight rules is a **vector space**, and *every theorem proved
from the eight rules alone* (independence, basis, dimension, eigen-everything) applies to
all of them at once. So the axioms are how a single proof covers $\mathbb{R}^n$ *and*
function spaces *and* the space of random variables simultaneously. We do not chase that
abstraction in this track — our payoff lives in $\mathbb{R}^n$ — but knowing the rules are
the portable interface is the graduate-level point.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run the two operations on real, honestly messy data — never a toy rigged to flatter
the idea. The first example combines feature vectors; the second builds a weighted average
and shows it *is* a linear combination; the third visits the edge, where forming a linear
combination is mechanically fine but *meaningless*.

## Example 1 — combining feature vectors into a score {#sec-score}

A common, genuinely used task: collapse several measured features into a single score by a
weighted sum. We take a small nutrition dataset — five cereals, each a vector of three
features (grams of sugar, grams of fiber, calories per serving) — and build a crude
"healthiness score" as a linear combination of the feature *columns*. Reward fiber, penalize
sugar and calories.

```{python}
#| label: compute-cereals
#| echo: false
names   = ["Bran Flakes", "Sugar Pops", "Oat Squares", "Choco Rings", "Plain Oats"]
sugar   = np.array([5.,  12.,  6.,  13.,  1.])   # grams
fiber   = np.array([5.,   1.,  4.,   1.,  4.])   # grams
calorie = np.array([90., 120., 100., 110., 150.])# kcal

# Each FEATURE is a column vector in R^5 (one entry per cereal).
# A score is a linear combination of these three feature vectors.
w_sugar, w_fiber, w_cal = -1.0, 2.0, -0.02
score = w_sugar * sugar + w_fiber * fiber + w_cal * calorie

print("weights:  sugar = -1.0,  fiber = +2.0,  calories = -0.02")
print(f"{'cereal':12s} {'sugar':>6s} {'fiber':>6s} {'kcal':>6s} {'score':>8s}")
for nm, s, f, c, sc in zip(names, sugar, fiber, calorie, score):
    print(f"{nm:12s} {s:6.0f} {f:6.0f} {c:6.0f} {sc:8.2f}")
```

The key reframing — the one that makes this an article about linear combinations rather than
about cereal — is *which vectors are being combined.* We treat each **feature** as a column
vector in $\mathbb{R}^5$ (one entry per cereal): $\mathbf{s}$ holds the five sugar values,
$\mathbf{f}$ the five fiber values, $\mathbf{c}$ the five calorie values. The score vector is
then the single linear combination
$$
\mathbf{score} = w_s\,\mathbf{s} + w_f\,\mathbf{f} + w_c\,\mathbf{c}
= (-1.0)\,\mathbf{s} + (2.0)\,\mathbf{f} + (-0.02)\,\mathbf{c},
$$
exactly the form of @def-lincomb with $k = 3$ vectors in $\mathbb{R}^5$. By the
componentwise rule @eq-lincomb-comp, cereal $j$'s score is
$-1.0\,s_j + 2.0\,f_j - 0.02\,c_j$ — the printed table is this formula evaluated in each of
the five slots at once. @fig-score draws the combination building up term by term for one
cereal and shows the resulting ranking.

```{python}
#| label: fig-score
#| fig-cap: "A score as a linear combination of feature vectors. **Left:** for one cereal (Oat Squares) the score is built by scaling each feature by its weight and adding — a waterfall from $0$ up through $-1.0\\cdot\\text{sugar}$, $+2.0\\cdot\\text{fiber}$, $-0.02\\cdot\\text{calories}$ to the final value. This is @eq-lincomb-comp in one slot. **Right:** the whole score vector — the same linear combination evaluated in all five slots — ranks the cereals. Changing the three weights would tilt the whole ranking; the weights *are* the coefficients of the combination."
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.6, 4.2))

# --- Left: waterfall for one cereal (index 2: Oat Squares) ---
j = 2
contribs = [w_sugar * sugar[j], w_fiber * fiber[j], w_cal * calorie[j]]
labels = [f"$-1.0\\cdot{sugar[j]:.0f}$", f"$+2.0\\cdot{fiber[j]:.0f}$",
          f"$-0.02\\cdot{calorie[j]:.0f}$"]
running = 0.0
colors_wf = [SCALED, ACCENT, GREY]
for k, (cb, lab, col) in enumerate(zip(contribs, labels, colors_wf)):
    axL.bar(k, cb, bottom=running, color=col, alpha=0.9, width=0.6)
    axL.text(k, running + cb / 2, lab, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=8, color="white")
    running += cb
axL.bar(3, running, color=VEC, width=0.6)
axL.text(3, running / 2, f"score\n$={running:.2f}$", ha="center", va="center",
         fontsize=9, color="white")
axL.axhline(0, color="black", lw=0.8)
axL.set_xticks(range(4))
axL.set_xticklabels(["sugar", "fiber", "kcal", "total"])
axL.set_ylabel("contribution to score")
axL.set_title("One score = scale each feature, add")

# --- Right: the full score vector, ranked ---
order = np.argsort(score)
axR.barh([names[i] for i in order], score[order],
         color=[ACCENT if score[i] >= 0 else SCALED for i in order], alpha=0.9)
axR.axvline(0, color="black", lw=0.8)
axR.set_xlabel("healthiness score")
axR.set_title("Same combination, all 5 slots")
plt.tight_layout()
```

The honest reading: the *score* is a genuine linear combination, and that is precisely why
it is interpretable — each weight is the marginal effect of one more gram (or calorie) on
the total, and doubling all weights doubles every score without changing the ranking (Rule 7
and Rule 5 at work). But it is also why the score is *only as good as the weights*: there is
no learning here, just arithmetic. Choosing the weights from data — so the combination
predicts something real — is regression, and the linear combination defined here is the
object regression optimizes over.

## Example 2 — a weighted average is a linear combination summing to one {#sec-weighted}

This is the example the roadmap calls out, and it is worth doing carefully because the
"sum to one" condition is the single most important special case of a linear combination in
all of data science. We take four monthly returns of a small stock portfolio — each month a
vector of three asset returns (in percent) — and ask for the portfolio's *blended* return
under a fixed allocation.

Suppose we hold $50\%$ in asset A, $30\%$ in asset B, $20\%$ in asset C. The portfolio
return in any month is
$$
\mathbf{r}_{\text{port}} = 0.5\,\mathbf{r}_A + 0.3\,\mathbf{r}_B + 0.2\,\mathbf{r}_C,
$$
where $\mathbf{r}_A, \mathbf{r}_B, \mathbf{r}_C \in \mathbb{R}^4$ are the four-month return
vectors of the three assets. This is a linear combination (@def-lincomb) with coefficients
$0.5, 0.3, 0.2$ — and notice they **sum to one**. That constraint is what makes it an
*average* (a "convex combination") rather than an arbitrary blend: the result is guaranteed
to land *between* the inputs, never outside their range.

```{python}
#| label: compute-portfolio
#| echo: false
months = ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr"]
rA = np.array([ 2.0, -1.5,  3.0,  0.5])   # asset A monthly returns (%)
rB = np.array([ 1.0,  0.5, -0.5,  2.0])   # asset B
rC = np.array([-1.0,  2.5,  1.0, -0.5])   # asset C

w = np.array([0.5, 0.3, 0.2])             # allocation weights, sum = 1
print(f"weights = {w.tolist()},  sum = {w.sum():.2f}")
r_port = w[0]*rA + w[1]*rB + w[2]*rC
print(f"{'month':6s} {'A':>6s} {'B':>6s} {'C':>6s} {'port':>8s}")
for m, a, b, c, p in zip(months, rA, rB, rC, r_port):
    print(f"{m:6s} {a:6.2f} {b:6.2f} {c:6.2f} {p:8.3f}")
print(f"\nrange each month: portfolio return lies between min and max asset return")
```

The componentwise rule @eq-lincomb-comp produces the portfolio column slot by slot; e.g. for
January, $0.5(2.0) + 0.3(1.0) + 0.2(-1.0) = 1.0 + 0.3 - 0.2 = 1.1$. We can now state the
general fact cleanly, and prove it, because it is the property that makes "sum to one"
special.

::: {#lem-convex}
**Lemma (a sum-to-one combination stays inside the range).** Let $a_1, \dots, a_k$ be real
numbers (one coordinate of each input vector), and let $c_1, \dots, c_k \ge 0$ with
$\sum_i c_i = 1$. Then the combination $\sum_i c_i a_i$ lies between the smallest and the
largest of the $a_i$:
$$
\min_i a_i \;\le\; \sum_{i=1}^{k} c_i a_i \;\le\; \max_i a_i.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
Write $m = \min_i a_i$ and $M = \max_i a_i$, so $m \le a_i \le M$ for every $i$. Multiply
the inequality $a_i \le M$ by $c_i \ge 0$ (multiplying an inequality by a nonnegative number
preserves its direction): $c_i a_i \le c_i M$. Sum over $i$:
$$
\sum_i c_i a_i \;\le\; \sum_i c_i M = M \sum_i c_i = M \cdot 1 = M,
$$
where we pulled the constant $M$ out of the sum and used $\sum_i c_i = 1$. The identical
argument with $m \le a_i$ multiplied by $c_i \ge 0$ gives $\sum_i c_i a_i \ge m \sum_i c_i =
m$. Hence $m \le \sum_i c_i a_i \le M$. Because this holds in every coordinate at once, the
*vector* combination $\sum_i c_i \mathbf{v}_i$ lies, coordinate by coordinate, inside the
box spanned by the inputs. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is precisely why a portfolio's return cannot beat its best asset or trail its worst
(no leverage, no shorting — those would be coefficients outside $[0,1]$ or summing past
one). It is also why the **mean** of $n$ data points,
$\bar{\mathbf{x}} = \frac1n \sum_i \mathbf{x}_i = \sum_i \tfrac1n \mathbf{x}_i$, is a linear
combination with all coefficients $c_i = 1/n$ — which sum to one — so the centroid always
sits *inside* the cloud, never outside it. @fig-weighted shows a two-asset blend sweeping as
the weight moves, tracing the line segment between the two input vectors.

```{python}
#| label: fig-weighted
#| fig-cap: "A weighted average traces the segment between its inputs. Two assets' (return, risk) profiles are the vectors $\\mathbf{a}=(3.0,\\,4.0)$ and $\\mathbf{b}=(1.0,\\,1.5)$. The convex combination $t\\,\\mathbf{a}+(1-t)\\,\\mathbf{b}$ — coefficients $t$ and $1-t$, summing to one — sweeps the full line *segment* between them as $t$ runs from $0$ to $1$ (dots, shaded by $t$). It never leaves the segment, the geometric face of @lem-convex: a sum-to-one, nonnegative combination stays inside the convex hull of its inputs. The equal-weight average $t=\\tfrac12$ (green) is the midpoint."
a = np.array([3.0, 4.0]); b = np.array([1.0, 1.5])
ts = np.linspace(0, 1, 11)
pts = np.array([t * a + (1 - t) * b for t in ts])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 4.4))
ax.plot([b[0], a[0]], [b[1], a[1]], color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.2, zorder=1)
sc = ax.scatter(pts[:, 0], pts[:, 1], c=ts, cmap="viridis", s=55, zorder=3)
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), a, color=VEC, lw=1.8, label=r"$\mathbf{a}$ ($t=1$)", label_dx=0.05)
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), b, color=SCALED, lw=1.8, label=r"$\mathbf{b}$ ($t=0$)", label_dx=-0.05, label_dy=-0.28)
mid = 0.5 * a + 0.5 * b
ax.scatter([mid[0]], [mid[1]], color=ACCENT, s=120, zorder=4,
           edgecolor="white", linewidth=1.4)
ax.text(mid[0] + 0.06, mid[1] + 0.05, r"equal-weight avg", color=ACCENT, fontsize=9)
ax.scatter([0], [0], color="black", s=22, zorder=5)
fig.colorbar(sc, ax=ax, label="weight $t$ on $\\mathbf{a}$")
ax.set_xlim(-0.3, 3.6); ax.set_ylim(-0.3, 4.6)
ax.set_xlabel("expected return"); ax.set_ylabel("risk")
ax.set_title("Convex combination stays on the segment")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What is the difference between a linear combination, an affine combination, and a convex
combination?"* All three are $\sum_i c_i \mathbf{v}_i$; they differ only in what the
coefficients are allowed to be — and the distinction matters constantly in practice. A
**linear** combination puts *no* constraint on the $c_i$ (they range over all reals); its
reachable set is a whole subspace through the origin ([GEOM-02]). An **affine** combination
adds the single constraint $\sum_i c_i = 1$; it reaches the flat through the points but
*not* through the origin, and it is the natural setting for "weighted average / interpolation"
(a regression's *prediction*, $\hat y = \sum \hat\beta_j x_j$, is affine in the inputs once
the intercept is counted). A **convex** combination adds *both* $\sum_i c_i = 1$ *and*
$c_i \ge 0$; by @lem-convex it stays inside the convex hull of the points, which is why means,
mixture weights, attention weights in transformers, and probability-weighted expectations are
all convex combinations. Being able to name which one a quantity is tells you immediately
where it can land.
:::

## Example 3 — the edge: combinations that are mechanically fine but meaningless {#sec-edge}

An honest article shows where the operation, applied blindly, produces nonsense. The two
operations are defined for *any* lists of equal length, so the arithmetic never complains —
but a linear combination is only *meaningful* when the coordinates are commensurable
quantities for which scaling and adding make real-world sense (the boundary GEOM-00 flagged
for vectors themselves, now inherited by combinations of them).

Consider a customer table whose columns mix true magnitudes with coded categories and an
identifier:

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge
#| fig-cap: "The edge of applicability. Two customer rows are added and averaged componentwise; the arithmetic runs without error, but only *some* of the resulting coordinates mean anything. **Age** and **income** combine sensibly (an average age is a real age). **Region code** (1=North, 2=South, 3=West) averages to $2.0$ — a number that points at 'South' purely by accident of the encoding; the label arithmetic is meaningless. **Customer ID** sums to a larger ID that identifies no one. The lesson: the two operations are always *defined*, but a linear combination is *interpretable* only on coordinates where adding and scaling have real meaning."
labels = ["age", "income\n($000)", "region\ncode", "customer\nID"]
cust1 = np.array([30.,  55.,  1.,  4001.])
cust2 = np.array([50.,  85.,  3.,  4007.])
total = cust1 + cust2
avg   = 0.5 * cust1 + 0.5 * cust2
meaningful = [True, True, False, False]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.4, 4.0))
x = np.arange(len(labels)); width = 0.28
for k, (vals, lab, off, col) in enumerate([
        (cust1, "customer 1", -width, VEC),
        (cust2, "customer 2", 0.0, ACCENT),
        (avg,   "average",    width, SCALED)]):
    bars = ax.bar(x + off, vals, width, label=lab, color=col, alpha=0.9)
ax.set_xticks(x); ax.set_xticklabels(labels)
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_ylabel("value (log scale)")
ax.set_title("Componentwise average: defined everywhere, meaningful only sometimes")
for xi, ok in zip(x, meaningful):
    ax.text(xi, 1.4, "OK" if ok else "nonsense", ha="center",
            color=(ACCENT if ok else SCALED), fontsize=10, fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9, loc="upper left")
plt.tight_layout()
```

The arithmetic in @fig-edge is flawless and useless in half the columns. Averaging two
region codes ($1$ and $3$) yields $2$, which the encoding happens to call "South" — but had
we coded the regions $\{1, 2, 4\}$ the same two customers would average to $2.5$, pointing
nowhere. The "average customer ID" identifies no customer at all. **This is the honest edge
of the linear combination: it is the right tool exactly when the coordinates are quantities
you can legitimately scale and add — magnitudes, counts, rates — and the wrong tool the
instant a coordinate is a label or an identifier in numeric clothing.** The fix is the one
GEOM-00 named: one-hot encode categoricals (so each category gets its own honest $0/1$ axis)
and exclude identifiers from the geometry entirely. The operation does not police its own
inputs; you must.

# Failure modes and honest boundaries {#sec-failure}

This article defines two operations, so its "failure modes" are the misuses of those
operations and the places the picture quietly misleads.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**A linear combination is only as meaningful as its coordinates, and only as honest as its
scales.** Three traps, the first two carried forward from GEOM-00 and now sharpened by the
combination itself:

- **Mixing categorical codes into a combination** (Example 3). Adding or averaging label
  codes produces a number with no referent. One-hot encode first.
- **Incommensurable scales.** Combining "income in dollars" (tens of thousands) with "age
  in years" (tens) in a single weighted sum lets income silently dominate, not because it
  matters more but because its *numbers* are bigger. Equal-looking weights are not equal
  influence until the features are standardized. This is benign here but becomes a genuine
  bug the moment a *distance* is computed ([GEOM-04]), where the large-scale feature drowns
  the rest.
- **Forgetting that "sum to one" is what makes an average an average.** A combination with
  coefficients that do *not* sum to one is a perfectly valid vector, but it is not a weighted
  average and need not lie among the inputs (@lem-convex needs the constraint). Reporting
  $1.2\,\mathbf{a} - 0.2\,\mathbf{b}$ as "a blend of A and B" is leverage, not averaging, and
  can land far outside the data.
:::

A second, subtler boundary concerns the *picture* versus the *algebra*. The tip-to-tail and
"slide along a line" drawings are faithful in $\mathbb{R}^2$ and $\mathbb{R}^3$, and they
*generate* correct intuition in higher dimensions — but, exactly as GEOM-00 warned, the
drawing is a crutch and the coordinate definition is the truth. A linear combination of
hundred-dimensional feature vectors is computed slot by slot by @eq-lincomb-comp whether or
not you can visualize it; trust the arithmetic, use the planar picture only to form
conjectures. When this article's combinations reappear as the columns of a matrix being
mixed ([GEOM-08]), there will be no picture at all for the high-dimensional case — only the
componentwise rule, which is why we were so careful to anchor every drawing to it here.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is the linear-combination view the right tool, and when does it break down?"* The
summary to have ready:

- **Use it** whenever you are blending, weighting, or mixing genuine magnitudes —
  combining features into a score, averaging observations into a centroid, allocating a
  portfolio, interpolating between states, mixing distributions. Its virtues are that it is
  interpretable (each coefficient is a marginal effect), it is the *only* structure linear
  algebra offers, and everything downstream (span, projection, matrices, regression) is
  built from it.
- **It breaks** when (a) a coordinate is a label or identifier rather than a magnitude
  (Example 3), (b) the true relationship is *non*linear — a product, a threshold, a
  saturation — in which case scaling-and-adding cannot represent it and you need feature
  transforms, kernels, or a nonlinear model, or (c) the features live on wildly different
  scales and you have not standardized, so the weights do not mean what they appear to.
- **The deepest framing:** "linear" *is* "expressible as scale-and-add." If a phenomenon is
  not, no choice of coefficients will rescue the linear combination — that is not a tuning
  problem, it is a modeling-class problem, and it is the boundary where this entire
  foundational track hands off to the nonlinear methods built later.
:::

# How you would actually compute this {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. You never loop over slots by
hand. In NumPy, addition and scaling are the operators `+` and `*` on arrays, applied
elementwise exactly as @eq-addition and @eq-scaling prescribe: `v + w` and `c * v`. A full
linear combination $\sum_i c_i \mathbf{v}_i$ is most cleanly a matrix–vector product —
stack the vectors as columns of a matrix `V` and multiply by the coefficient vector `c`,
`V @ c`, which is precisely the "matrix takes a linear combination of its columns" identity
of [GEOM-08] that this article sets up. The convenience function `np.average(X, axis=0,
weights=w)` computes a weighted average (a convex combination) directly and even normalizes
the weights to sum to one for you — a reminder that the "sum to one" constraint of
@sec-weighted is common enough to be baked into the library. Whatever the call, the
operation underneath is the two definitions of this article, done slot by slot.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[GEOM-00 *What Is a Vector, Really?*]**, which fixed the object — the
vector as arrow, list, and data row — and repeatedly used "componentwise addition and
scaling" on credit. This article defined and drew those two operations, fused them into the
**linear combination**, and proved the eight rules they obey are just real-number arithmetic
done one coordinate at a time. From here the build continues:

- **[GEOM-02 *Span and Subspaces*]** — the immediate next step: turn the coefficients of a
  linear combination *loose* over all of $\mathbb{R}$ and ask *which points you can reach*.
  The answer (the span) is always a flat through the origin (a subspace). Everything in
  GEOM-02 is the @def-lincomb of this article, quantified over its coefficients.
- **[GEOM-03 *Linear Independence, Basis, and Dimension*]** — asks when the vectors in a
  combination carry redundant information (one is already a linear combination of the
  others) and what the smallest non-redundant set is.
- **[GEOM-04 *The Dot Product*]** — finally equips $\mathbb{R}^n$ with **length and angle**,
  so the "stretch" of scaling and the "closeness" of combinations become measurable numbers.
- **[GEOM-08 *Matrices as Linear Maps*]** — the payoff promised throughout: a matrix acts on a
  vector by forming a **linear combination of its columns**, with the vector's entries as
  coefficients. The single most useful reading in the track is this article's @def-lincomb
  in matrix clothing.

The running thread the track never drops: **a dataset is a cloud of points, and the linear
combination is the one move by which we build new points from old** — every later technique
is this move, repeated and constrained in different ways.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The two operations as the *definition* of the subject.** Any modern linear-algebra text
  (Strang's *Introduction to Linear Algebra*; Axler's *Linear Algebra Done Right*) opens
  with these two operations and the eight rules; Axler in particular treats the rules as the
  abstract interface (@thm-vector-space) and develops the entire theory from them with
  minimal reference to coordinates — the portable-interface view of the "You might be asked"
  box in @sec-rules.
- **Convex combinations, taken seriously.** The sum-to-one, nonnegative case (@lem-convex) is
  the entry point to **convex geometry** — convex hulls, convex sets, and convex
  optimization — which underpins much of the Optimization track and the theory of why
  least-squares and many learning problems have unique, findable solutions.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *Which points can a fixed set of
  vectors reach, and what shape is that set?* — [GEOM-02]. *When is one of the combined
  vectors redundant?* — [GEOM-03]. *How do we measure the length a scalar stretches an arrow,
  and the angle between two combined vectors?* — [GEOM-04]. *Why is "take a linear combination
  of columns" the same thing as "multiply by a matrix"?* — [GEOM-08]. Each is a direct
  continuation of a promise made above.
